title: Andy Burnham gagne et veut diriger le pays
gist: Andy Burnham won a big election and wants to become Britain's prime minister, replacing Keir Starmer. He wants to spend on public services, but investors worry he'll spend too much when the UK's finances are already tight — so many think taxes may have to rise. Business leaders just want a stable government.

body.fr: Andy Burnham {{gagne|wins}} une {{élection importante|big / important election}} au Royaume-Uni.

bd.meaning: Andy Burnham wins a big election in the United Kingdom.
chunks: Andy Burnham wins — present tense, used for fresh news
a big election ('importante' comes AFTER the noun and takes -e to agree)
in the United Kingdom (au = à + le)
bd.key: Adjective after the noun, and agreeing with it: importante follows élection (feminine), so it takes an -e.

bd.meaning: He wants to become Prime Minister.
bd.key: Helper + plain verb: veut devenir = 'wants to become'. Only veut changes; devenir stays plain.

bd.meaning: He wants to replace Keir Starmer.
bd.key: Same helper shape again: veut remplacer — vouloir + plain verb. You'll meet this pattern constantly.

body.fr: Avant, Burnham {{a été|was / has been}} {{maire|mayor}} de Manchester.

bd.meaning: Before, Burnham was mayor of Manchester.
chunks: was mayor (a été = avoir + été, the past)
bd.key: Past = avoir + past form: a été = 'was / has been' — the same avoir-past you know (a gagné, a inventé). Note: no 'un' before a job.
populaire already ends in -e, so it looks the same for a man or a woman — no extra -e to add.

body.fr: Burnham veut {{dépenser|spend}} de l'argent pour les {{services publics|public services}}.

bd.meaning: Burnham wants to spend money on public services.
chunks: Burnham wants to spend (vouloir + plain verb)
for public services ('publics' after the noun, plural)
bd.key: The 'some' word: de l'argent = 'money'. French adds de l' / du / des where English says nothing.
An avoir idiom: French 'has fear' = 'is afraid'. avoir peur, like avoir faim (be hungry) — it uses avoir, not être.

body.fr: Ils pensent qu'il va {{dépenser trop|spend too much}}.

bd.meaning: They think he's going to spend too much.
chunks: they think that (que becomes qu' before a vowel)
he's going to spend (aller + plain verb = future)
bd.key: aller + plain verb = the future: va dépenser = 'is going to spend'. Only va changes.

body.fr: Le problème : le Royaume-Uni {{a emprunté|borrowed}} beaucoup d'argent.

bd.meaning: The problem: the UK has borrowed a lot of money.
chunks: a lot of money (beaucoup de becomes d' before a vowel)
bd.key: Past again: a emprunté = 'borrowed'. And beaucoup d'argent — 'de' shrinks to 'd'' before the vowel.

body.fr: Il y a {{peu de place|little room}} pour dépenser plus.

bd.meaning: There is little room to spend more.
bd.key: Two set pieces: il y a = 'there is/are', and pour + plain verb = 'in order to' (pour dépenser = 'to spend').

bd.meaning: On the markets, rates go up.
chunks: the (interest) rates — 'taux' is spelled the same singular and plural
bd.key: Present tense for live action: les taux montent = 'rates are rising' — the simplest way to report what's happening now.

body.fr: Burnham est un homme {{de gauche|left-wing / on the left}}.

chunks: a man of the left / a left-wing man
bd.key: 'de + noun' builds a description: un homme de gauche = 'a left-wing man' (the opposite is de droite, right-wing).

chunks: he doesn't like (the ne...pas sandwich around 'aime')
bd.key: Negation sandwich: ne ... pas wraps the verb aime; ne becomes n' before the vowel.

body.fr: Alors, beaucoup pensent que les {{impôts|taxes}} vont {{augmenter|go up, rise}}.

bd.meaning: So, many people think taxes are going to rise.
chunks: taxes are going to rise (aller + plain verb)
bd.key: Future with aller, plural: vont augmenter = 'are going to rise'. Plural subject, so aller becomes vont.

bd.meaning: Burnham promises to lower energy bills.
bd.key: Some verbs link with 'de': promet de baisser = 'promises to lower' — the linking word is 'de', then the plain verb.

chunks: a stable government ('stable' sits after the noun)
bd.key: vouloir = 'to want': plural is veulent. And the adjective stable comes after the noun.

grammar.en: aller + plain verb = the near future. It recurs all through this story. Only aller changes (va, vont); the second verb stays plain.
avoir + past form = the past. a été = 'was', a emprunté = 'borrowed'. Same shape as the vibe-lawyering story.
avoir idioms. French says 'has fear' for 'is afraid' (avoir peur) — like avoir faim (be hungry). Uses avoir, not être.

quiz.a: Parce qu'ils pensent que Burnham va dépenser trop d'argent. (Because they think Burnham is going to spend too much money.)

flag: This was a dense economics piece compressed hard to A1: I dropped the specialist terms (deficit, bond yields, fiscal rules, the chancellor race, the named advisers). Les taux montent stands in for 'bond yields pushed up' — close enough at this level, not a literal match. It's a neutral retelling of your article, not my own view on UK politics.
